speaker: interviewer
text: --survivor is Gertrude Weiss and interviewer is [? Sandy ?] Jacobson.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, so now you have made it home.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You've finally gotten home. Is there anybody living in your house when you get home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Our house is completely bombed. Satu Mare was a city which was heavily bombed, because it was somehow the front-- they-- they were fighting there. They-- there was a-- they put up a front there that was bombed. But we rebuilt it. As I mentioned, my grandfather had a-- was a big landowner in Batarci. And after his passing, the seven children inherited the land. So it was divided into seven. But my family got two portions because of the two mothers. So we had a lot of land. And a cousin, which was a man who was in forced labor camp-- he wasn't deported-- was home quite a while before with my husband. And they started the-- activate the mill. The mill started to-- to work at home. But they had tremendous business, because the mill was closed and the peasants didn't have anything to eat. They were-- they were parking there with their wagons for days until their turn-- turn came to-- to make the grain into flour in that mill. So-- so-- and then land was leased to the-- to the village as a pasture. So he had money for us. So from that money, we rebuilt the house. We certainly didn't bring any money from Auschwitz. We rebuilt the house, but we never lived in it. Because we didn't want to live in it. We leased it out.

speaker: interviewer
text: You and your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And then I got married. Yes, I came home sick. And I found home a friend who was a daughter of was one of the three families who were exempt-- [INAUDIBLE] very lovely girl-- and her parents. And we were taken to the hospital straight when we got home. There was a Jewish hospital in Satu Mare-- a well-equipped, relatively good hospital. We were taken there. And they came to pick me up there. And they took me to their house. So I stayed with them for a few days. Then my cousin from Batarci came in-- came to Satu Mare and picked me up and took me to Batarci. So he had-- they had a house here-- house there. This was an uncle who was the-- who owned the mill. And the partner was my husband's father. Now, the two boys came home, and they continued the partnership. And they were the ones who-- who had the mill. And they had a big house, had a relatively big house in Batarci. So all the Jews who passed there or-- or in the surrounding villages-- you know, one member of a family got home in this village, another member from another family, you know, so they didn't have where to stay-- they all stayed in that house. And they had a couple of woman who cooked. And it was, you know, like a community thing there. So he took us to Batarci. And we stayed in Batarci until I got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, had there been any romance between you and your husband earlier? You said you knew him. Was this somebody you were sort of interested in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. I was pretty young at that time. I had two older sisters at home. In our Jewish Orthodox way of life, and-- and back, you know, in the beginning of the 1900s, you know, you had to wait your turn. That was-- that was a big problem, a big calamity, something not happy if a younger one married before the older one. And usually the girls had to marry before the boys. You know. But-- so I didn't really think-- and then let me mention something. I was the city girl who went to [? Richelm, ?] which was, as I mentioned, not a common occurrence because of the religious, you know, aspect of it. And-- so he was a boy from-- from the village, you know. And I didn't before But after, we were in the same house, then yes, there was romance before-- before we got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then you got married, and you continued to live in Batarci?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We lived in Satu Mare. I did not want to live in Batarci. He commuted. He went every Monday morning to Batarci to attend the business. And he came back for Shabbos. We lived in Satu Mare. We had a very nice furnished apartment-- not furnished, we bought the furniture. And I lived with my sister-- that was natural-- and also a cousin of his who came home and who had no parents. This was an everyday occurrence that you lived with extended family, because there was no family. You know, those who got married, who-- who made a home-- and that's where-- that's where we lived.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you decide to emigrate?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't decide. We were forced. The Communists came and again, misery and no future. Most Jews left. We stayed on almost too long because of the mill. The mill was a fantastic business. We couldn't sell anything. We left the mill, my house-- my rebuilt house, our land, everything. We-- we had some money, because my husband was in business. Everything was converted into dollars, because our currency, was-- there was no sense to. And it was hidden in some unbelievable body parts. And-- and there were-- it was a frequent thing, because it happened frequently, that there were a few people got together. And usually a gentile man who knew the border smuggled us over the border. Because we couldn't legally get out from Romania. And in order to get to America or Israel, or you had to get out. So we got together my sister, of course, and this cousin, and some other people who we knew. We were about 11 in the group. And again, left-- we had already a home. My son-- my oldest son was born in Satu Mare. And we left everything. And we got out. Again, I'll never forget that night. We had to walk in the night though to cross the border. And it was a long walk. We had to-- we had to travel by train to another city, a smaller city than Satu Mare, which was on the border. And from there, by foot we were taken. And the-- and the ground on what we walked, it was a farming area. It was a sandy area. I remember they-- they grew there melons. And our feet just sank in. And I lost my shoes. I wound up, finally, without shoes, you know. But wait-- I-- we had already a child. We had a baby. And the baby, we went to our pediatrician. We were very close to our pediatrician, because I had no idea when I became a mother how to handle a baby. Where I grew up, there were no babies in our home. And I never had any. And there was no-- no mother. There was no aunt. There was no older sister. There was nobody with some experience. With every diaper, really, I ran to the pediatrician. Because he-- he did have some problems. And we told him of our plan. He was a Jewish, Dr. Stein. And he suggested-- he gave Peter some sleeping potion. And we bought a big basket with two handles. And we made a bed. And my husband and I carried him.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was he?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a year and something. And we were-- when we were in the open fields, the child got cramps and started to scream. This-- this is really a separate story of ours. The man, the gentile who was the guide, came to us. And he said, if you don't turn back, I'll kill you, because you are endangering the whole group. And we turned around. The rest continued to Hungary, because that's where we were headed for. The rest continued to Hungary. And we turned back without shoes, with a crying child who was a mess-- we tried to feed him something-- and without money. Because the money couldn't-- we couldn't get our hands on-- on the money. And we got back that city to the railway station. That-- you know, in Europe at that time-- maybe today too. I don't know. In France, where I was, I don't see. There were-- it was very common to be stopped by a police or a gendarme and to ask for identification. And those times were especially such.

speaker: interviewee
text: So anybody who looked at us could see that-- what-- what our situation is. So we were shivering, naturally. So my husband approached a man, took off his watch, and told him-- it was golden watch-- give me money for two tickets to-- from that-- from that town to get back to Satu Mare. So that's-- that's how we bought tickets.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we were extremely lucky again. The gendarmes and the police boarded the trains every-- to-- to-- to identify-- to-- to identify people, you know--

speaker: interviewer
text: Check papers.

speaker: interviewee
text: --on the trains to check people. I don't know how we managed to get back to Satu Mare. But we had no home. We had nowhere to go into. We went in to this cousin, I told you, to this lady was with us in-- in Zittau, who was my mother's age. But we were not welcome, because it was very dangerous to-- to give refuge to people like us. Because if she would have been suspected or caught somehow, it's-- it's-- it's a very big problem. So in a couple of weeks, we arrange for another transport-- for another guide. And we did. The doctor gave us some other medication. And we did cross the border. But as we stepped on Hungarian soil, the gendarmes, the police over there caught us. It's was really very awful. And they-- it was a border town, a small town there. And they kept us in a kind of jail. But they-- they let me out with Peter to walk in the yard a little. They took everything away. We had-- we had gold jewelry. My husband had a gold cigarette holder. Because they said that, you know, it's good to buy gold, because gold you could use, you know. So.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they took everything away. We gave everything just. And they let us contact-- my husband had a cousin in Debrecen. That's a major city in Hungary. And [? Moscowitz ?] was his name, who-- who made the arrangements for somebody to come down to that down, and who made arrangements for money that they let us out. And we wound up in Budapest. And from Budapest?

speaker: interviewee
text: We lived in Budapest for-- with my sister there. My sister continued to Budapest with the first transport. We met our sister and our cousin there. We lived in a-- in a furnished room by a woman who made a living out of-- out of that, you know. They charged not a normal rent, of course, for people of-- in these circumstances. You know, because it was dangerous, again.

speaker: interviewer
text: And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there were people who made the false passports. We wanted to get to Czechoslovakia. And from Czechoslovakia, the Bricha-- you know what the Bricha is? You must know. The Bricha took us to Germany. So my husband, a lot of money, $220-- it was a fortune at that time, you know, practically-- paid for one passport. And the passport, imagine, was done that I was his daughter. And my sister who was older was his wife. And that little boy was-- Your brother.

speaker: interviewee
text: So imagine. That's why he crossed-- that's how we crossed the border. I still, you know, was young. And I wasn't much developed. And he grew a mustache, and he should look older because we didn't have money for two passport. It was a lot of money. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So these details are just unbelievable for a-- for an average American. I mean, it's just so-- we got to Budapest. No?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we got to Czechoslovakia. And from Czechoslovakia, already the Joint. You know the Joint? The Jewish organization who helped every-- everybody during the war. The Joint housed us in a hotel. And we-- and we-- we were displaced persons, you know. And through the Bricha, we were taken to Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because from Germany, you were-- you could come to America. Back to Germany-- Germany, it was organized. They-- they were helpful in-- in-- they were helpful with-- they helped the Jews to-- to-- to get-- to get out. They processed papers. And they did everything. But you had to have a certificate that you were in a certain date in-- in-- in Germany. You were not allowed to leave Germany. You were supposed to be there, and then they--

speaker: interviewer
text: You had to establish a [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: And then they helped you. And they processed papers. But there was a quota. There was a-- so you had to wait sometimes three year or two years in Germany until you were able to--

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you wait?

speaker: interviewee
text: --get out. We waited over a year.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you survived? You lived--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, over there, the Joint, the UNRRA and the Joint-- food packages. And we were-- we were in a camp, which was-- we were provided by the UNRRA but managed by Jews. There was a committee.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name? Was it like a name-- did it have a name, the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, yes, we were in Gabersee-- G-A-B-E-R-S-- double-E, Gabersee.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then from there, you finally got your papers?

speaker: interviewee
text: From there, we got papers. And through-- we-- we the left from Bremen-- that's a major port in Germany-- by boat by was the name of the-- of the ship. And we came to America. And we land-- we got to Boston-- Boston Harbor. And from-- we were in Boston for a few days longer than usual, because Peter got the chickenpox. But let me tell you something. I don't-- the trip took a few days. Because it was-- I was pregnant with my second son. The food was-- was good compared to-- although in Germany, we-- we had regular food and everything. And-- but I shall never forget that the bread-- the first time that I had something like that, that Wonder Bread, which is so white, and so soft, and so sweet. And you know, me pregnant, I just couldn't look at it, not to eat it. I-- I-- you know, on the ship you are anyway nauseous. So it was-- it wasn't an easy time. And we could hardly wait. And we were up all night to get a glimpse of the shore of America. I'll never forget that. And Peter is sick. And they don't let me be next to him, because it was contagious. And imagine the dawn-- we saw the sunrise. It was something-- it was something, the most joyous moment in our lives, but Peter was taken to the hospital. And they told us that he's going to be taken to the hospital. Imagine arriving in America, in the new world, and-- and rising from the ashes-- we-- we rose like the phoenix. And-- and here, my child is taken away. We were housed by a rabbi. And this date-- it was during fall. We stayed for Rosh Hashanah by the Boston rabbi. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was '47?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think. And-- and we were walking every day to the hospital two hours one way to see Peter. But they didn't let me in. I could see him only through the window and was standing there and cry. And when he was taken-- and when he was taken away, he was screaming in Hungarian, my shoes, my shoes! [SPEAKING HUNGARIAN] The child-- I don't know why he was afraid for-- for his shoes. He was-- that was something. That was a terrible beginning, because, you know, a young mother with only child.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we stayed longer until he was released. And then we came to New York, where my husband had an aunt and an uncle-- no, two uncles and an aunt. And he worked there?

speaker: interviewee
text: He-- we stayed in New York for a very short time, because we had here also an uncle and an aunt. And they wanted us here. And my husband's older brother-- they are three brothers-- [? Bessie's ?] husband, who is the oldest. And we have a younger brother, David Weiss, a younger brother-in-law. And they were already in Los Angeles. Because this aunt had no children. They were called Mr. and Mrs. [? Louis ?] [? Walter. ?] And they were very, very warm, very helpful. So we came. We were only for a short time in New York. We came to Los Angeles. So I usually say we are almost natives.

speaker: interviewer
text: And here you had a third son.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. The second son was born here.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was pregnant. And he was born in the Queen of Angels Hospital, and [? they were-- ?] of course I remember. And then it was Bobby born here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, why don't we meet your son, who's come to visit you, all right?

speaker: interviewee
text: It will be a pleasure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wait a second.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please introduce us to your son.

speaker: interviewee
text: It is my pleasure to introduce my son number two, Jerry Weiss.

speaker: interviewer
text: Jerry, it's very nice to meet you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Pleasure to be here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your mother. Tell me what kind of a mother you had, as a woman who had lived such a difficult life experience to have lost so many people that she loved, and lost her home. Was she a bitter mother to grow up with?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not at all. Although I knew of no other circumstance other than growing up in a family that survived the war, not only immediately in the home, but virtually all of my childhood friends were in the same situation. I never felt the consequence of that, other than in passing discussion. It was never made a-- it was never highlighted. It was never made a significant aspect of our lives. And I think, as I recall, that it wasn't even made a significant aspect of their lives. In other words, we didn't hear about it on any regular, constant basis. I think as we got older and learned about what it meant, we asked the questions and got brief responses. So to say bitter is the-- it goes without saying. But even more so, I don't think other than the fact that we grew up in an environment that missed grandparents and relatives that probably other families had-- although my close contemporaries did not. And so that wasn't something that was missing, but it was just a fact of life that existed. I don't think the consequence was hit home on a conscious, obvious level at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, let me ask you how you could do that, how you could raise a family after all that you'd been through and not-- and not teach them the horrors of what you'd experienced?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is so interesting. When I spoke with you on the telephone, I said that I would like to interject some things. One point what I had in mind-- it was my father, but this even more so-- that when I became a mother, I was very concerned how my children are gonna go up. Because it was an unnatural environment. It was-- it never existed in modern times that a child shouldn't normally have grandparents or older aunts, uncles, relatives. But since we were many in the same boat, and we were busy with our everyday lives, which wasn't easy-- which we-- we all started a new life in a new world. It-- and one more thing-- that for many years already, I am a very-- and I'm very proud that under these circumstances, we brought that children who are very, very normal and-- and-- and accomplished like-- like just a small percentage of society at large. And under-- and I am very proud of that fact-- that among us, there were no more emotional problems or-- or-- or family problems than-- than society at large, even-- even less because we lived that Orthodox Jewish way of life, which-- which, you know, guides. And-- and we have a certain path to follow. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me just ask you about that. After all that you had seen, your belief was never shaken?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- oh, I am sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I mentioned to you on the telephone that many of our type who-- who came from very Orthodox home and survived did have problems and did stray from the Orthodox, from our path. But us-- this was the way we knew. This was the way what we wanted. This was life. This was security. This was-- It never occurred to-- to-- to stay away from it, although it-- it-- it was much more difficult in the new world under our circumstances of life would have easier and simpler not to observe Shabbat strictly and the rest of the kosher laws and everything. But this was the life what we wanted. And this was what we followed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you very much.
